one_liner: Rating politicians' promises by how impossible they are.
search_doc:
Rating politicians' promises by how impossible they are.
Mechanisms: data-as-advocacy, proprietary-index-creation, legislative-advocacy, political-accountability-data
Idea: For the first time, ASDRA decided to expose the candidates' proposals through data analysis, building an indicator capable of measuring the implementation difficulty of each candidate's proposals: the Complexity Index.
Insight: Politicians' absurd electoral promises about disability can be exposed by treating them as data and ranking their unfeasibility.

extracted.tagline: We analyzed the most unreal proposal to create a new law.
extracted.context: In Argentina, people with Down syndrome must annually renew their disability certificate due to an absurd law ignored by politicians.
extracted.idea: For the first time, ASDRA decided to expose the candidates' proposals through data analysis, building an indicator capable of measuring the implementation difficulty of each candidate's proposals: the Complexity Index.
execution: Analyzed candidate proposals during the 2023 presidential election
Built a 'Complexity Index' indicator measuring implementation difficulty
AI-powered analysis combining economists, political scientists, lawyers, journalists and peers
Quantified the complexity of the most unusual proposals
Media partnership with mdz news outlet

inferred.insight: Politicians' absurd electoral promises about disability can be exposed by treating them as data and ranking their unfeasibility.
inferred.one_liner: Rating politicians' promises by how impossible they are.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used a data-built Complexity Index to expose unfeasible political promises about disability, helping push through a new disability law in Argentina benefiting 5 million people.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial board with desaturated portrait photographs of Argentine politicians framed in red rectangles, with two huge green percentage figures dominating the centre and a semi-circular legislative-chamber dataviz at the bottom. Dense info-graphic layout with multiple text blocks.

idea.rationale: Building a proprietary 'Complexity Index' that ranks politicians' promises by how impossible they would be to implement is a clean turn of advocacy into a data product, with the index itself becoming a re-usable accountability artefact. The mechanic — rate the promise, not the politician — gives press a fresh angle that lecture-style disability advocacy doesn't earn.

strategy.rationale: ASDRA is the Argentine Down Syndrome Association, an advocacy NGO whose stated mission is full inclusion and rights of people with Down syndrome — making a data tool that forces electoral candidates to defend (or fail) on disability policy is precise mission-fit, not borrowed activism. The context is data-backed and verifiable: Argentina's disability certificate renewal law has been a documented grievance of the community for years, and the 2023 presidential cycle was a real-world inflection point for the cause. The Complexity Index is built from a credible methodology stack (economists, political scientists, lawyers, journalists, peers) and an AI layer, which gives the data move source-veracity rare for an NGO entrant.

execution.rationale: The campaign sequences from index construction through a media partnership with mdz news outlet, into election-window coverage and ultimately a legislative push that lands a new disability law. Channel choice is correct for the audience — political journalists and legislators, not the general public — which is why the index format (a journalist-ready dataset) rather than a public-facing app is the right artefact. The thinness of the press partnership (a single named outlet) is the soft spot.

impact.rationale: The real business outcome for a disability-rights NGO is legislative change, and the board names it directly: 'New disability law approved' — the rare case where a Creative Data Lion ties impact back to policy passage rather than impressions. The '+5 million people impacted' is the size of Argentina's disability-affected population, a credible reach denominator, while the headline percentages — '645%', '1953%', '100% benefited' — are unanchored and lack absolute bases (645% of what coverage, against what baseline?), so they read as soft proof beside the law itself.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because the Complexity Index converts an NGO grievance into a journalist-ready dataset that helped land an actual disability law — a rare Creative Data case where the data move ends in legislation, not impressions.

one_liner: Dogs' heartbeats become an earthquake early warning.
search_doc:
Dogs' heartbeats become an earthquake early warning.
Mechanisms: biometric-data-as-sensor, real-time-data-driven-creative, data-as-product, geolocation-targeting
Idea: PetPace partnered with London South Bank University to develop Animal Alerts — the first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data, using integrated media to alert residents in real time.
Insight: There is scientific evidence that animals change their behavior even hours before an earthquake.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/SILVER/Animal Alerts - Petpace (Serviceplan Munich _ L&C New York).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data.
extracted.context: In 2023, 66,900 people died in earthquakes, many of which were felt in Lima, Peru — located on the Pacific Ring of Fire, where 90% of the world's seismic activity occurs. Even with today's technology, people still can't predict an earthquake.
extracted.insight: There is scientific evidence that animals change their behavior even hours before an earthquake.
extracted.idea: PetPace partnered with London South Bank University to develop Animal Alerts — the first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data, using integrated media to alert residents in real time.
execution: Smart collars measure temperature, pulse and heart-rate variability of dogs
Algorithm calculates stress level in real time and flags abnormal collective behaviour
Partnered with local pet stores to recruit pet owners and dogs in Lima
Alerts sent via geo-targeted mobile notifications, banners, digital OOH and radio
Earthquake warning broadcast across mobile, billboard and radio channels

inferred.one_liner: Dogs' heartbeats become an earthquake early warning.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a pet-health wearable into a public earthquake-alert infrastructure for Lima, alerting 9M residents and detecting 18 seismic events via collective canine biometrics.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dark hero shot of a black Weimaraner wearing the PetPace collar with red waveform graphics emanating behind it, anchoring a symmetrical info-graphic layout with text blocks, app and OOH mockups, a Peru map and press logo strip. Dense, polished editorial style.

idea.rationale: Converting collective canine biometrics into an earthquake early-warning signal is a striking biometric-data-as-sensor reframe — pets stop being a product audience and become a distributed seismic network. The mechanic answers a real-world impossibility (people still can't predict earthquakes) by routing around it through a different species' anomaly detection.

strategy.rationale: PetPace is a US-Israeli smart-collar company whose mission is monitoring pet vital signs to detect early illness, so repurposing its biometric stream as a public seismic-alert network is mission-adjacent rather than directly mission-fit — the brand becomes a civic-infrastructure provider, which is purpose-stretch territory. The scientific premise (that animals exhibit pre-seismic behavioural change) is widely reported in folk and academic literature, but remains contested and the board cites it as 'scientific evidence' without naming a study; partnering with London South Bank University adds an academic anchor but the underlying causal claim is the soft spot. Lima's position on the Pacific Ring of Fire and the '90% of seismic activity' framing are verifiable cultural-geographic facts.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is well-layered: smart collars feed temperature, pulse and HRV into an anomaly-detection algorithm; pet-store partnerships recruit the canine sample base in Lima; alerts then fan out across geo-targeted mobile, DOOH, banners and radio at the moment of need. The multi-channel alert architecture is correct for an emergency-warning use-case where redundancy matters. The thinness is in cohort transparency — the board does not disclose how many collared dogs constitute the Lima sensor network.

impact.rationale: The two headline numbers are partially anchored: '+9M residents alerted' is the reach denominator (roughly Lima's metropolitan population) and '18 seismic events detected' is an absolute count, but the board does not show false-positive rate, lead-time-before-quake, or any documented case of an alert saving lives — the business outcome an early-warning system must ultimately demonstrate. Press coverage is broad (Forbes, CNN, Mashable, EL PAÍS, Fast Company) but is entrant-reported and unaudited, and the case rests heavily on the prestige of the press logo wall rather than on a verified lives-impacted figure.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because turning a pet wearable into a city-scale seismic sensor is a genuinely original data reframe, but the impact rests on reach and press-logo prestige rather than a verified lead-time-before-quake or lives-impacted number.

one_liner: The bandage that tells you your blood type.
search_doc:
The bandage that tells you your blood type.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, diagnostic-as-media, data-as-product, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Blood-Aid is a new kind of band-aid, which has a fine layer that in seconds when in touch with blood can detect what type of blood that is. Making the information of blood type readily available to everyone who comes for blood donation. This is Blood-Aid.
Insight: Most people don't know their blood type, and not knowing is itself the biggest barrier to giving blood when it matters.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/BRONZE/Blood Aid - Dasa Integrated Health System (DM9, Sao Paulo).jpeg

extracted.tagline: A fast, new, cost effective and scalable way to reveal people's blood type.
extracted.context: In Brazil, 40% of the population is unaware of their blood type. Knowing your blood type is important for several reasons. First, because some health factors are more common to specific blood types. Second, because when you donate your blood type you can donate when your specific blood type is in short supply and in need.
extracted.idea: Blood-Aid is a new kind of band-aid, which has a fine layer that in seconds when in touch with blood can detect what type of blood that is. Making the information of blood type readily available to everyone who comes for blood donation. This is Blood-Aid.
execution: Microporous adhesive tape outer layer
Synthetic fiber middle layer
Blood-reactive liquid that reveals the blood type
Tested across the major blood groups O-, O+, A-, A+, AB-, AB+, B-, B+
Distributed at blood donation drives

inferred.insight: Most people don't know their blood type, and not knowing is itself the biggest barrier to giving blood when it matters.
inferred.one_liner: The bandage that tells you your blood type.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reinvents the household bandage as a low-cost diagnostic tool that reveals a wearer's blood type, lowering the friction to know and donate at scale.

visual.style_description: Editorial product-design board with a tight close-up photograph of a forearm wearing the round Blood-Aid sticker showing 'AB+', set on a soft beige and grey palette with cell-like dot motifs. Right column shows exploded diagrams of the three product layers; bottom strip shows eight skin shots tagged by blood type.

idea.rationale: Reinventing the bandage as a blood-typing diagnostic is a tight everyday-object-transformation: the moment of a small wound becomes the moment of learning a critical health fact. The product reads in one image — a sticker on a forearm showing 'AB+' — which gives the idea instant clarity on the board.

strategy.rationale: Dasa is Latin America's largest integrated diagnostic-medicine network (laboratories, imaging, hospitals), so a low-cost blood-typing tool is precise category-fit for a diagnostics group whose business IS knowing patients' biological data. The context is sourced and specific ('40% of Brazilians don't know their blood type' is presented as a population-level statistic, plausible against Brazilian Ministry of Health and Hemocentro communications). Where the Strategy axis weakens is in the data dimension required by THIS category: the work is a physical-product invention more than a Creative Data move — there is no proprietary dataset, no analytic insight, no real-time data layer; the 'data' is the single piece of information the bandage reveals to its wearer, which is thin for Creative Data.

execution.rationale: The product engineering — microporous adhesive outer, synthetic fibre middle, blood-reactive liquid — is described in just enough detail to read as a real prototype, and the test across eight major blood groups (O-/+, A-/+, AB-/+, B-/+) shows the diagnostic range. Distribution at blood-donation drives is the right point-of-need channel, but the board does not show the campaign phasing, amplification, or partner-network rollout that would turn a prototype into a population-scale tool.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics and no press coverage, which is structurally fatal for a Creative Data Lion whose weight on Impact is 0.35. There is no documented behaviour change (donations triggered, blood-types learned, units of blood collected attributable to the device), no production-scale figure, and no third-party validation — the case rests entirely on the product concept and the asserted population gap. Without the data-to-outcome chain the work cannot demonstrate that the proprietary diagnostic actually moved the system it was designed to fix.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the bandage-as-diagnostic invention is a clean everyday-object transformation native to a diagnostics group, but the work lives mostly as a product concept — the data layer is thin and there is no documented impact, both critical gaps in the Creative Data category.

one_liner: Your gaming account is now collateral for a loan.
search_doc:
Your gaming account is now collateral for a loan.
Mechanisms: data-as-collateral, gamer-credit-scoring, category-redefinition, financial-inclusion-mechanic
Idea: Young gamers may not have recognised how valuable that their video game accounts can be used as collateral for a loan.
Insight: Gamers spend traditional assets, young Ecuadorians possess a valuable asset in what they control: their video game accounts.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/SILVER/Gamer Loan - Banco Del Pacifico (Paradais DDB, Guayaquil).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Changing the way gamers experience banking.
extracted.context: Only 3 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan because they are unable to afford what banks require as collateral.
extracted.insight: Gamers spend traditional assets, young Ecuadorians possess a valuable asset in what they control: their video game accounts.
extracted.idea: Young gamers may not have recognised how valuable that their video game accounts can be used as collateral for a loan.
execution: Banco del Pacifico became the first bank to validate young Ecuadorians' loan applications using their video game accounts (Clash of Clans, Pokemon Go, Minecraft) as collateral
Pre-approved loans using gaming data such as account level and inventory value
Designed a verification process linking gamer profiles to banking applications
metrics: 7 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan
$282,956 pre-approved in first week of campaign

inferred.one_liner: Your gaming account is now collateral for a loan.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-engineered a bank's credit-scoring model so that in-game assets count as collateral, lifting loan access for young Ecuadorians and driving a 248% spike in loan requests in week one.

visual.style_description: Neon-lit hero portrait of a young woman in a hoodie and headset holding a game controller with a glowing $5,321 USD on-screen amount; large 'THE GAMER LOAN' wordmark in cyan above, four game-account thumbnail cards with valuation labels across the centre, two text columns (Problem / Opportunity / Solution) and three pink-and-cyan metric blocks at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Treating in-game inventory and account level as bank-grade collateral is a clean category-redefinition that expands what a financial institution will accept as a creditworthy asset. The mechanic is unmistakable in a single line — your Clash of Clans account is now a loan guarantee — which gives the work easy purchase in both gaming and banking conversations.

strategy.rationale: Banco del Pacifico is one of Ecuador's largest universal banks, and a credit-inclusion product for young Ecuadorians sits within mainstream commercial bank purpose, though the youth-and-gaming angle is more challenger-positioning than a long-standing brand mission. The context is data-anchored ('3 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan' is a specific exclusion statistic), and the insight — that gamers hold genuine financial-grade assets in their accounts — is plausible because secondary markets do price game inventory. The veracity gap is in collateral-recovery enforceability: how does a regulated bank actually seize a video-game account on default? The board does not address this load-bearing operational question.

execution.rationale: The mechanic links three games (Clash of Clans, Pokémon Go, Minecraft) into a pre-approval pipeline that scores account level and inventory value, with a verification flow that bridges gamer profiles into the bank's loan application stack. Channels are not detailed beyond the product itself, and there is no documented press-and-amplification layer on the board — the campaign appears to live mostly on the strength of the product launch rather than an orchestrated multi-phase rollout.

impact.rationale: The business outcomes are partially anchored: '$282,956 pre-approved in first week' is an absolute dollar amount, which is the right kind of evidence for a lender; '7 out of 10 young Ecuadorians can access a loan' is framed as the post-intervention rate but the methodology behind that ratio is not shown. The headline '+248% increase in loan requests' lacks an absolute base — 248% of how many baseline applications, over what comparison window — and reads as a soft uplift rather than a hard sales figure. No press coverage is listed on the board, so the cultural-conversation layer is unproven.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because re-engineering credit scoring to accept gaming assets is a credible category-redefinition for an Ecuadorian bank, but the operational reality of seizing in-game collateral and the absent press loop keep the case from a higher tier.

one_liner: MRI noise becomes the soundtrack of a children's story.
search_doc:
MRI noise becomes the soundtrack of a children's story.
Mechanisms: data-as-storytelling-medium, noise-as-narrative, medical-anxiety-reframe, audio-product-as-media
Idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to create a unique library of audiobooks to turn scary MRI noises into a wondrous experience. Each Magnetic Story is connected to the sounds of the MRI scan, using familiar MRI scan noises and turning these dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
Insight: Children's fear of the MRI scanner stems from its loud, unfamiliar noises; if those exact noises can be reframed as the soundtrack of a story, the scan becomes a listening experience rather than a frightening one.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/GOLD/Magnetic Stories - Siemens Healthinners (Area 23, An Ipg Health Network Comoany, New York).jpg

extracted.tagline: The most common noise sequences of MRIs turned into beautiful children's audiobooks.
extracted.context: MRI sounds can be louder than a military jet. The keys are scary for children and they present a barrier to think for the radiology team.
extracted.idea: Siemens, a top MRI manufacturer, partnered with children's book authors to create a unique library of audiobooks to turn scary MRI noises into a wondrous experience. Each Magnetic Story is connected to the sounds of the MRI scan, using familiar MRI scan noises and turning these dreadful sounds into a delightful narrative.
execution: Collected the most common MRI sequences and used them as a time-coded music score
Audio was added to a Magnetic Story corresponding to the sounds of one specific MRI scan
Stories distributed as an audiobook collection that gives kids a journey to listen to while the scan is being done
Vacations matching up with the sound sequence in patient timing

inferred.insight: Children's fear of the MRI scanner stems from its loud, unfamiliar noises; if those exact noises can be reframed as the soundtrack of a story, the scan becomes a listening experience rather than a frightening one.
inferred.one_liner: MRI noise becomes the soundtrack of a children's story.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframes the scariest part of a paediatric MRI - the noise - as the spine of a custom audiobook, turning a clinical pain point into a brand-building wellness product for Siemens Healthineers.

visual.style_description: Cinematic hero photograph of a young girl lying inside an MRI scanner wearing headphones, with glowing pink and orange sound-wave streaks radiating from her head; central serif title 'Magnetic Stories' over the image, text columns labelled Problem / Idea / Execution beneath, and a strip of six waveform thumbnails along the bottom mapping story titles to MRI noise types.

idea.rationale: Treating MRI noise sequences as a time-coded musical score and writing children's audiobooks ON TOP of those exact noises is a category-precise data-as-storytelling move — the scary input becomes the narrative spine. It's the rare Creative Data idea that converts proprietary engineering data (machine noise patterns) into a consumer-grade emotional product.

strategy.rationale: Siemens Healthineers is one of the world's largest MRI manufacturers, and the brand's positioning around 'pioneering breakthroughs in healthcare. For everyone. Everywhere.' makes paediatric MRI anxiety a precise mission-fit problem to solve at the source — not borrowed purpose. The context is verifiable on its face (MRI scanner noise can exceed 100 dB, comparable to industrial machinery, which is widely documented in radiology literature) and paediatric scan anxiety is a documented clinical problem that delays scans and forces sedation. The insight — that the noise itself can be reframed as the soundtrack of a story — is plausible and clinically actionable, though the board does not cite radiology-team adoption data.

execution.rationale: The mechanic is precise: collect the most common MRI sequences, treat them as a time-coded score, then commission children's authors to write stories whose narrative beats land on the actual scanner noises a child hears during the scan. Distribution as an audiobook library handed to radiology teams is the right channel — point-of-anxiety, not a marketing campaign — which makes the work native to the clinical workflow rather than bolted on to it.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics and no press coverage, so the impact case rests entirely on the craft of the product itself rather than on documented behaviour change in scan completion rates, sedation reduction, or paediatric anxiety scores — the obvious business outcomes for a paediatric MRI intervention. For a Creative Data entry the absence of any quantified data-to-outcome chain is the structural weakness, even when the product concept is strong.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because the work converts the scariest part of paediatric MRI — the noise itself — into the data-spine of a custom audiobook, a precise mission-fit product from the MRI maker; held below higher tiers by the absence of any quantified clinical outcome on the board.

search_doc:
Turtle soup without the turtle.
Mechanisms: ai-as-chef, data-as-product, endangered-species-substitute, tradition-preserved-without-harm
Idea: NotCo, a tech food brand known to create plant-based versions of milk and chicken, created NotTurtle, a first-of-its-kind endangered animal dish without the endangered animal — made to save the turtles' declining population while also providing an alternative to communities where turtle soup is a tradition. NotTurtle tastes exactly like turtle soup at a molecular level, thanks to NotCo's AI chef, Giuseppe, which can replicate the texture and taste of anything by analysing its database of 300,000 plants.
Insight: Cultural traditions like turtle soup will not disappear by lecturing people; the only way to remove the demand on endangered species is to engineer an indistinguishable plant-based copy and hand it back to the same chefs.

extracted.tagline: The first endangered animal dish without the endangered animal. Created by NotCo's AI for good.
extracted.context: 100,000 turtles are killed every year to make turtle soup, a tradition in many countries that drives turtles to extinction.
extracted.idea: NotCo, a tech food brand known to create plant-based versions of milk and chicken, created NotTurtle, a first-of-its-kind endangered animal dish without the endangered animal — made to save the turtles' declining population while also providing an alternative to communities where turtle soup is a tradition. NotTurtle tastes exactly like turtle soup at a molecular level, thanks to NotCo's AI chef, Giuseppe, which can replicate the texture and taste of anything by analysing its database of 300,000 plants.
execution: AI chef 'Giuseppe' replicated turtle-soup texture and taste from a 300,000-plant database
Documentary feature on National Geographic
The entire journey was documented and crafted into content; reached telepathic viewers and trailers globally on Instagram, TikTok and YouTube
Every execution raised awareness about the turtle issue, the prowess of Giuseppe and most importantly, open-sourced the recipe to chefs and restaurants with a NotTurtle cooking class
press_coverage: Forbes — "Imagine what else we can do with this technology"
Wired — "A culinary innovation with a for the preservation of sea turtles"
CNN — "A turtle soup is saying an endangered species"

inferred.insight: Cultural traditions like turtle soup will not disappear by lecturing people; the only way to remove the demand on endangered species is to engineer an indistinguishable plant-based copy and hand it back to the same chefs.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used a 300,000-plant AI database to clone an endangered-animal dish at molecular level, generating over 1 billion impressions and 95% positive sentiment while open-sourcing the recipe to chefs.

visual.style_description: Photoreal hero close-up of a steaming bowl of brown 'turtle' soup on a dark slate background, with the green crossed-out turtle 'NOT TURTLE' wordmark at top-left and the National Geographic logo top-right; below the bowl are three small research / soup / classes thumbnail strips on the left, three large metric callouts in the middle, and three press-quote logos along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Building 'the first endangered-animal dish without the endangered animal' by molecular-level AI matching is a memorable conservation-via-substitute reframe — instead of lecturing communities to abandon tradition, the work engineers a copy and open-sources the recipe. The 'NotTurtle' wordmark with the crossed-out turtle carries the idea in one beat.

strategy.rationale: NotCo is a Chilean alt-protein food-tech brand whose mission is replicating animal-derived foods at molecular level via its proprietary AI 'Giuseppe', so a plant-based turtle-soup is direct mission-fit — the work IS what the company does, applied to an endangered-species case. The 300,000-plant database is a real proprietary asset that gives the data move source-veracity. The strategic weakness is in the data-as-insight axis Creative Data rewards: the underlying dataset is genuinely proprietary, but the brief's load-bearing claim — '100,000 turtles killed every year for soup' — is asserted without source on the board, and the 'turtle soup is a tradition driving extinction' framing is broad rather than locally precise (the practice has very different scales in different cuisines). Per RIGOUR pattern 5, the cultural-scope precision is thin.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs from AI recipe generation, through a National Geographic documentary that gives the work credentialed-conservation framing, into open-sourcing the recipe via NotTurtle cooking classes for chefs and restaurants — which is a thoughtful tail that hands the substitute to the actual community of practice. Social distribution on Instagram, TikTok and YouTube provides the trailer-and-trailers awareness layer. The campaign phases are present but lightly described.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers are surrogate metrics rather than business or conservation outcomes: '+190 publications' and '+1 billion impressions' are entrant-reported reach claims (RIGOUR pattern 2), and '+95% positive sentiment' lacks a base — 95% of how many measured mentions, scored by which methodology. The real business outcome for a conservation-via-substitution product is documented displacement: turtles not killed, restaurants that swapped to NotTurtle, soup volumes shifted — none of which appears on the board. The press quotes from National Geographic, Forbes, Wired and CNN are credible breadth but support awareness, not behaviour change.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the molecular-AI substitute idea is on-brand and the National Geographic loop gives it conservation credentials, but the impact case is share-of-voice rather than documented displacement — no turtles-saved or restaurant-adoption figure is shown.

one_liner: Nature's data tells us where to act first.
search_doc:
Nature's data tells us where to act first.
Mechanisms: data-as-storytelling-medium, proprietary-data-newsmaking, behavioural-data-pattern-revealed, purpose-driven-product
Idea: In Belgium, biodiversity institutions and public actors measure the quality of air, water, and soil. However, they do so in individuality and separately. With 'Pulse of Nature', we have gathered all this data via a single platform. After analysing this data, a synthetic indicator is created showcasing a clear status of the planet's health.
Insight: Environmental data exists in Belgium, but lives in disconnected silos — air, water and soil are measured separately. Aggregating them into one synthetic indicator lets nature itself rank what needs fixing first.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/BRONZE/Pulse Of Nature - Veolia (Famous Grey Brussels).jpg

extracted.tagline: For the first time nature helps us define its priorities.
extracted.context: As a leader in ecological transformation, Veolia develops customised solutions to help businesses reduce their impact on the environment. But how can we help nature act more effectively?
extracted.idea: In Belgium, biodiversity institutions and public actors measure the quality of air, water, and soil. However, they do so in individuality and separately. With 'Pulse of Nature', we have gathered all this data via a single platform. After analysing this data, a synthetic indicator is created showcasing a clear status of the planet's health.
execution: Tool aggregates Belgian biodiversity datasets (air, water, soil) into a single platform
Synthetic indicator visualises ecological priorities as a coloured map
Interactive web platform and mobile app
Map ranks top local priorities (e.g. nitrogen, organic carbon, mineral pollution)
metrics: +934% more traffic to the Veolia website
press_coverage: This map really helps us to see how we can act locally. — Enrico Caironi, CEO Vivaqua
Finally we know where to start. — Pasquale De Gregorio, Co-Founder Companii

inferred.insight: Environmental data exists in Belgium, but lives in disconnected silos — air, water and soil are measured separately. Aggregating them into one synthetic indicator lets nature itself rank what needs fixing first.
inferred.one_liner: Nature's data tells us where to act first.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Aggregated Belgium's fragmented biodiversity data into a single priority map; drove +934% website traffic, +321% interest and 1 million earned media impressions while shifting how local actors plan ecological action.

visual.style_description: Aerial photograph of a river and farmland overlaid with a grid of coloured pixel-tiles representing ecological data points; right column holds yellow callout metric blocks, bottom row shows two phone mockups of the app and a five-icon 'top priorities' legend.

idea.rationale: Aggregating Belgium's siloed air, water and soil biodiversity datasets into a single synthetic ecological priority indicator is a sensible 'nature ranks what to fix first' move. The mechanic is honest and useful but lives squarely inside the established dataviz-aggregator pattern — the originality lift over existing environmental dashboards is modest.

strategy.rationale: Veolia is the French global utility leader in water, waste and energy services, marketing itself as 'the reference in ecological transformation', so building a tool that helps Belgian businesses and public actors prioritise ecological action is direct mission-fit — the dataset is also the sales territory. The context is sourced and verifiable (Belgian biodiversity institutions do measure air, water and soil independently — this is a real fragmentation). The structural soft spot is data-source veracity for THIS category: the work aggregates third-party public datasets rather than generating proprietary research, which is the lighter end of Creative Data — the 'data move' is integration, not discovery, and the underlying datasets' cohort sizes and methodologies are not surfaced on the board.

execution.rationale: The artefact is a coloured ecological-priority map with a phone app counterpart and a five-icon legend ranking nitrogen, organic carbon, mineral pollution etc. — the right format for a B2B-and-public-sector audience. Channels are described thinly: the work appears to live primarily as a destination platform plus an earned-media press push, without a documented multi-phase rollout or partner-coalition layer that would extend its reach beyond the launch window.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers are uniformly percentage uplifts without absolute bases — '+934% traffic to Veolia website', '+321% increase in interest', '+16.2% top of mind awareness', '1 million earned media' (RIGOUR pattern 1): 934% of what baseline traffic, 321% of which interest measure, 16.2 points off what starting awareness in which segment? These read as soft impressions-and-traffic claims rather than as documented behaviour change in ecological action commissioned, contracts won, or biodiversity outcomes shifted — the business outcomes a utility brand in 'ecological transformation' should ultimately demonstrate. The two CEO quotes are warm but are entrant-curated testimonial, not third-party validation.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the priority-map aggregator is on-mission and useful for Veolia, but the data move is third-party integration rather than proprietary discovery and every impact metric is an unanchored percentage uplift.

one_liner: Every surf session becomes a global competition.
search_doc:
Every surf session becomes a global competition.
Mechanisms: wearable-data-as-competition, retroactive-event-creation, personalisation-at-scale, data-as-product
Idea: We picked a 10-day competition window completely in hindsight, collected surf data from everyone around the world who wore the watch, and using a unique algorithm turned their data into a score. We let the surfers know that they were in the Virtual Pro as their score appeared in a global competition leaderboard. Entrants were provided personalised visualisations to share. Rip Curl athletes shared their stories so they could even compete against them. The comp ran for 10 days with a winner — a manager in the surfing calendar and turned local surfers into a global community.
Insight: Every surfer with a Rip Curl GPS watch is already producing competition-grade performance data — they just don't know they're competing.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/BRONZE/Rip Curl Virtual Pro - Rip Curl (VML Sydney).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first global surf comp no one knew they were a part of.
extracted.context: Rip Curl's Search GPS watch has been tracking surfers' performance data for over a decade. And while it had limited itself to individual performance for all these years, Rip Curl found the opportunity to transform surfers' data into a way to compete with the rest of the world.
extracted.idea: We picked a 10-day competition window completely in hindsight, collected surf data from everyone around the world who wore the watch, and using a unique algorithm turned their data into a score. We let the surfers know that they were in the Virtual Pro as their score appeared in a global competition leaderboard. Entrants were provided personalised visualisations to share. Rip Curl athletes shared their stories so they could even compete against them. The comp ran for 10 days with a winner — a manager in the surfing calendar and turned local surfers into a global community.
execution: Used Rip Curl Search GPS watch sensors to gather wave length, wave speed, wave size and distance paddled data
10-day backdated competition window using all watch wearers' surf sessions
Algorithm scored each session against the global field
Personalised surf-stats visualisations shared by entrants
Pro Rip Curl athletes shared their results so surfers could compete with them
Global Virtual Pro leaderboard with a single winner declared

inferred.insight: Every surfer with a Rip Curl GPS watch is already producing competition-grade performance data — they just don't know they're competing.
inferred.one_liner: Every surf session becomes a global competition.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a decade of dormant smartwatch surf data into a backdated global competition for 1,671 surfers, repositioning Rip Curl's wearable as a community-scale ranking engine.

visual.style_description: Bold action-photography board with a hero shot of a surfer riding inside a curling wave, layered behind massive purple chrome 'Virtual Pro' lettering. Right side shows the Rip Curl watch interface; bottom strip carries phone-screen mockups, metric callouts and small portrait videos. Energetic, sports-magazine layout.

idea.rationale: Retroactively declaring a global surf competition from a decade of dormant smartwatch GPS data — and telling participants only AFTER they competed — is an inventive retroactive-event-creation mechanic. The 'first global comp no one knew they were a part of' line carries the idea cleanly.

strategy.rationale: Rip Curl is an Australian surf-apparel and surf-hardware brand whose 'The Search' platform has anchored its identity around endless surf-travel and surf-community for decades, so converting its proprietary Search GPS watch data into a community competition is direct mission-fit. The proprietary data asset (over a decade of wave length, wave speed, wave size and paddle-distance telemetry) is genuinely owned by the brand, which is the right kind of dataset for Creative Data. The strategic soft spot is consent and insight veracity: the work scores surfers who did not opt into a competition, raising a soft data-ethics question the board does not address, and the 'these are competition-grade sessions' insight is asserted rather than backed by comparison with sanctioned WSL contest data.

execution.rationale: The mechanic is technically clean — back-cast 10-day window, algorithmic scoring of every watch wearer's sessions against the global field, personalised stats visualisations shared back to entrants, and Rip Curl sponsored athletes posting their results so the community could measure itself against the pros. Personalisation-at-scale is the right execution layer for the data set. The thinness is in amplification — the board lists no press coverage, suggesting the campaign lived inside the surf community rather than breaking into mainstream sport conversation.

impact.rationale: The headline metrics are small absolute numbers (43,388 waves, 5,042 sessions, 1,671 surfers, 1 winner) which is honest reporting but a tiny participation base for a 'global' competition — the cohort size makes the move feel like a proof-of-concept rather than a scaled outcome. There is no business outcome shown — no Rip Curl Search watch sales lift, no app downloads, no community-membership growth, no earned-media coverage — which is the basic data-to-business chain Creative Data demands. The case rests on the elegance of the mechanic rather than on documented behaviour change.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the retroactive-comp-from-dormant-data mechanic is genuinely original and on-brand for Rip Curl, but the 1,671-surfer base is small, there is no documented business outcome, and the consent question around scoring people without their knowledge is unaddressed.

one_liner: The map that turns competitors into neighbours.
search_doc:
The map that turns competitors into neighbours.
Mechanisms: data-as-product, proprietary-data-newsmaking, behavioural-data-pattern-revealed, purpose-driven-product
Idea: The Room for Everyone campaign brings together Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs to open complementary businesses. We created a digital tool that combines transactions, footfall and business complementarity data to help them find mutually beneficial locations.
Insight: Mastercard data revealed a different story: many businesses are complementary, meaning they thrive better when located near one another. This was an opportunity to bring Ukrainians and Poles together.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE DATA/GRAND PRIX/Room For Everyone - MasterCard (McCann Warsaw).webp

extracted.context: The Russian invasion has displaced 1.55 million refugees into neighbouring Poland, increasing the country's population by 4%. With 10% of newly-opened businesses being Ukrainian, refugees were increasingly seen as competition.
extracted.insight: Mastercard data revealed a different story: many businesses are complementary, meaning they thrive better when located near one another. This was an opportunity to bring Ukrainians and Poles together.
extracted.idea: The Room for Everyone campaign brings together Ukrainian and Polish entrepreneurs to open complementary businesses. We created a digital tool that combines transactions, footfall and business complementarity data to help them find mutually beneficial locations.
execution: Digital tool combining transactions, footfall and business complementarity data
Map-based location finder for complementary businesses
OOH and digital posters listing wanted complementary partners (e.g. 'Travel agency wanted next to my Polish bakery')
Pairing examples: pet stores near drug stores, bookstores near jewellers, barbershops near restaurants, grocery stores near hairdressers, bakeries near beauty salons
metrics: Up to 40% of new business owners have used our tool
+10% positive attitude towards Ukrainians, reversing negative trend

inferred.one_liner: The map that turns competitors into neighbours.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Polish-Ukrainian business competition as complementarity using Mastercard transaction data; up to 40% of new owners used the tool and attitudes toward Ukrainians improved 10 points, reversing a negative trend.

visual.style_description: Dark aerial map of Warsaw with coloured location pins marking complementary business pairings; large phone mockup centre-right shows the in-app finder; OOH photo strip across the top displays staff holding 'wanted' partner signs.

idea.rationale: Reframing the dominant 'refugee-as-competitor' narrative as a complementarity map is a category-redefining use of payments data — the dataset itself becomes the social product. The 'wanted: travel agency next to my Polish bakery' creative artefact lands the idea instantly and unmistakably from a single OOH unit.

strategy.rationale: Mastercard is a global payments network whose 'Priceless' / 'Doing Well by Doing Good' platform has long anchored the brand to community-building uses of its transaction infrastructure, making proprietary spend and footfall data the only credible source for a complementarity insight — exact brand-mission fit, not borrowed purpose. The framing cites verifiable cultural facts (1.55M Ukrainian refugees in Poland, +4% population, 10% of new businesses Ukrainian-owned) which are publicly documented, and the complementarity finding (e.g. pet stores near drug stores) is grounded in Mastercard's own anonymised transaction data rather than an asserted hunch. The 'businesses thrive better when located near complementary peers' insight is the rare Creative Data move where the data IS the story.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs from a working transaction-and-footfall finder tool, through OOH 'wanted partner' posters that turn local entrepreneurs into media, to the press loop in Business Insider and Euronews that takes the dataset out of Poland and into a European conversation. Channels are correctly matched to the dual target — Ukrainian and Polish small-business owners reached at street level via OOH, and the digital tool delivering the match-making at the moment of opening a business.

impact.rationale: The headline behaviour number — 'up to 40% of new business owners have used the tool' — is the right business outcome for a complementarity finder, though 'up to' softens it and the absolute count of new owners is not disclosed on the board. The '+10 points positive attitude towards Ukrainians, reversing a negative trend' is anchored as a directional shift rather than a free-standing claim and matches the social objective directly. Press coverage in Business Insider and Euronews is entrant-reported but credible breadth for an earned-media signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because proprietary transaction data is rebuilt into a working complementarity tool that reframes Polish-Ukrainian business competition as cooperation — the data IS the social product, not a metric attached to a campaign.

one_liner: A music video that plays inside a spreadsheet.
search_doc:
A music video that plays inside a spreadsheet.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, data-as-storytelling-medium, b2b-sales-tool-as-creative, spreadsheet-as-canvas
Idea: Spreadbeats. A never-been-done-before, ear and eye-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We took our trailer's audio and tracks summit and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheets Spotify's salesforce sends to its clients, turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
Insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising salesforce sends out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their lovely good spreadsheets.

extracted.tagline: The first music video entirely coded and experienced in Excel.
extracted.context: In 2023, Spotify launched new video ad units. But there's a problem: media planners and advertisers still consider us as just an audio platform. And audio only commands 2.6% of overall ad spend. As a result, when reaching out for proposals, they often request media plans tailored exclusively for audio ads. That's a shame, because audio ads combined with video on Spotify are 2.2 times more effective than audio ads alone.
extracted.insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising salesforce sends out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their lovely good spreadsheets.
extracted.idea: Spreadbeats. A never-been-done-before, ear and eye-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio in a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We took our trailer's audio and tracks summit and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheets Spotify's salesforce sends to its clients, turning rows and columns into a canvas that sprang to life when the file was opened.
execution: Coded a four-minute music video as cell colour fills inside a real Excel spreadsheet
Sent as the salesforce's standard media-plan attachment to clients
1 million data points turned into art
All within one Excel file of 50MB
Turning built-in Excel functions into cutting-edge visuals
Inviting media planners to talk about Spotify's video ad units
Personalised for each recipient
metrics: 1 million data points turned into art

inferred.one_liner: A music video that plays inside a spreadsheet.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reached media planners by smuggling a coded music video inside the exact Excel spreadsheets Spotify's salesforce already sends them, turning a sales artefact into the proof of Spotify's video advertising case.

visual.style_description: Pixelated 8-bit aesthetic on a bright lime-green grid background; the hero is a laptop screen showing an Excel sheet whose cells form a magenta-and-yellow music-video frame, with 'SPREADBEATS' rendered as blocky pixel type above and four short labelled text columns (Challenge / Context / Idea / Origins) running down the left side, plus a thumbnail strip of additional screens along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Coding a four-minute music video as cell-colour fills inside the exact Excel spreadsheet Spotify's salesforce already sends to media buyers is a tight platform-jailbreak — the medium IS the message, and the artefact lands in the recipient's inbox as both proof and pitch. The spreadsheet-as-canvas mechanic is unmistakable and natively B2B.

strategy.rationale: Spotify is the global audio-streaming platform whose business problem here is precise and well-defined: video ad units launched in 2023 but media planners still treat Spotify as audio-only, and audio commands only 2.6% of overall ad spend versus a documented 2.2x effectiveness uplift when audio is combined with video on the platform — these are specific, anchored data points that source the strategic case credibly. The insight that media planners live in spreadsheets and receive thousands of Spotify proposals annually is observable and a tight fit for the channel choice. Where Strategy weakens for THIS category: the 'data' in Spreadbeats is functionally a creative-engineering trick (1M cells driven by code) rather than a proprietary research insight or a behavioural dataset — for Creative Data the data-as-engine question is answered thinly.

execution.rationale: The craft is high and channel-native: a 50MB .xlsx file with one million data points choreographed via built-in Excel functions to play the trailer audio's tracks, delivered as the standard salesforce attachment and personalised per recipient. The execution is the work — the spreadsheet is simultaneously the media plan, the demo, and the asset — which compresses the sales loop into a single artefact and makes the channel-target fit unusually tight.

impact.rationale: The board lists no business outcomes — no documented uplift in video ad sign-ups, no reported share-of-spend shift from media planners who received the spreadsheet, no awards-aside earned media, no recipient response rate. The two metrics shown ('1 million data points turned into art', '50MB Excel file') are description of the artefact rather than outcomes. For a B2B sales-enablement piece the obvious business outcome is conversion — media plans booked with video included — and its conspicuous absence (RIGOUR pattern 3) is the structural weakness. The case rests entirely on the craft of the artefact, not on documented sales movement.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the spreadsheet-music-video is a genuinely original B2B platform-jailbreak with high craft, but the data layer is a coding trick rather than a proprietary insight and no sales outcome is documented — the case is artefact-only.

one_liner: The Football Manager gamer becomes a real-life tactician.
search_doc:
The Football Manager gamer becomes a real-life tactician.
Mechanisms: data-as-product, simulation-as-recruitment, platform-jailbreak, behavioural-data-pattern-revealed
Idea: For the launch of Football Manager 2024, Xbox helped the National League tap into a new pool of talent (gamers). Football Manager 24 uses live data to replicate the real game, testing gamers tactics. Xbox turned the game into a recruitment tool to find a top gaming talent as the new tactician for professional team Bromley FC. The tactician gaming community went wild for the chance to apply, with 1.5B views on the job ad, and one gamer got the job. The tactician used game data to advise the club in real life, leading Bromley FC to achieve their highest league position ever and recent defeats in the league. Our documentary changed perceptions of how football talent is recruited and showed how Xbox powers your dreams.
Insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many spend hundreds of hours a month engaging in Football Manager.

extracted.tagline: Blurring the lines between simulation and the real world.
extracted.context: Football is flourishing in the Premier League, the richest league in the world. They have the best talent, and data, which is transforming the sport. But four leagues below, in the National League, teams are struggling and being left behind.
extracted.insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many spend hundreds of hours a month engaging in Football Manager.
extracted.idea: For the launch of Football Manager 2024, Xbox helped the National League tap into a new pool of talent (gamers). Football Manager 24 uses live data to replicate the real game, testing gamers tactics. Xbox turned the game into a recruitment tool to find a top gaming talent as the new tactician for professional team Bromley FC. The tactician gaming community went wild for the chance to apply, with 1.5B views on the job ad, and one gamer got the job. The tactician used game data to advise the club in real life, leading Bromley FC to achieve their highest league position ever and recent defeats in the league. Our documentary changed perceptions of how football talent is recruited and showed how Xbox powers your dreams.
execution: Football Manager 24 used as live data simulator to recruit a real tactician
Job advert calling for an 'Everyday Tactician' for Bromley FC
Selected gamer joined Bromley FC as real-life tactician using game data
Documentary film capturing the recruitment and season
metrics: 190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox
Best season in Bromley's 130 year history

inferred.one_liner: The Football Manager gamer becomes a real-life tactician.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a video-game simulation into a real recruitment pipeline for a struggling fourth-tier football club; 1.5B impressions, 190% gamer uplift on Xbox and Bromley's best season in 130 years.

visual.style_description: Dramatic cinematic portrait of a young Black gamer-tactician seated pitchside holding an Xbox controller, with stadium press-quote callouts floating in the upper corners; metrics tower to the right and a thumbnail strip plus press logo row at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Using Football Manager's live-data simulation as an actual recruitment pipeline for a real fourth-tier football club is a clean simulation-as-recruitment reframe — the game stops being a game and becomes a credentialing engine. The 'gamer becomes the real-life tactician' headline carries the idea in one beat and is built for press.

strategy.rationale: Xbox is Microsoft's gaming platform brand, and Football Manager is a flagship simulation title launching its 2024 edition — making the work primarily a product-launch vehicle dressed in social-purpose framing, where the brand mission ('Xbox powers your dreams') is asserted on the board rather than load-bearing. The context is partially data-backed (Premier League uses elite performance data; National League is under-resourced, a known structural fact in English football), but the insight that 'football fans think they could manage a team and play Football Manager for hundreds of hours' is observational rather than data-derived — for a Creative Data entry that is the structural soft spot, because the proprietary data layer (FM's simulation engine) is the product itself rather than a unique research finding.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is multi-layered and built for press: a public job ad calling for an Everyday Tactician, the FM-driven selection process, the live season at Bromley FC, and a documentary film that turns the season into long-form content. Channels span sports media (BBC, Match of the Day, The Athletic, FourFourTwo, BT Sports), tabloids (Daily Mail, Metro), youth-culture (LADbible, JOE, Hypebeast) and tech (techradar) — correct breadth for the dual gaming-and-football target.

impact.rationale: '190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox' and 'Most played FM ever' are credible product-launch outcomes for the actual business objective (drive Game Pass usage and FM sessions on Xbox), though 190% lacks an absolute base. '1.5B campaign impressions' is large but entrant-reported and unaudited per RIGOUR pattern 2 — reach claims of that order from a Premier-League-adjacent press loop deserve scepticism. 'Best season in Bromley's 130 year history' is a colourful supporting fact but the causal link to the tactician's contribution (versus other club factors) is not isolated on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the simulation-as-recruitment mechanic is genuinely original and the press loop landed at scale, but the proprietary-data layer is essentially the game itself rather than a unique research insight, and the headline impressions are unverified.

search_doc:
Empty roads become pop-up playgrounds.
Mechanisms: data-as-product, real-time-data-driven-creative, platform-jailbreak, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Introducing Turf Finder. In partnership with Google Maps, we used their historic insights tool to find when roads go empty, using locations the BMC had specifically allocated as 'recreational' but were taken over by traffic. That usability is measured not just by space, but by space and time. Our turfs were modular, easy to set-up, easy to dismantle. So the cities got their turfs, even back after games.
Insight: Usability of urban space isn't just about square metres — it's about time. Roads that are jammed by day go empty at night, and Google's historic traffic data can find those hidden windows where play is possible.

extracted.tagline: Turf Finder unlocks the future of play.
extracted.context: Mumbai is an island city, home to 22 million. It has a paucity of all of its parks/turfs, the existing ones small and far from majority of Mumbaikars. This has led to 70% of its population using 'lack of space' as the reason they don't engage in any physical activity.
extracted.idea: Introducing Turf Finder. In partnership with Google Maps, we used their historic insights tool to find when roads go empty, using locations the BMC had specifically allocated as 'recreational' but were taken over by traffic. That usability is measured not just by space, but by space and time. Our turfs were modular, easy to set-up, easy to dismantle. So the cities got their turfs, even back after games.
execution: 150+ new turf locations identified via Google Maps tools
20+ new turf locations allocated by BMC
Partnership with Government of India
Modular turfs set up on roads during empty hours
Reclaimed reserved 'recreational' BMC land for play
metrics: 150+ new turf locations identified by Maps tools
20+ new turf locations allocated by BMC

inferred.insight: Usability of urban space isn't just about square metres — it's about time. Roads that are jammed by day go empty at night, and Google's historic traffic data can find those hidden windows where play is possible.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used Google Maps' historical traffic data to convert temporarily empty Mumbai roads into modular sports turfs, identifying 150+ new play locations and unlocking 121% growth in volumes for Gatorade.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of a Gatorade-branded modular turf laid over an aerial road photograph with neon orange court markings; right side carries the headline and a sparse metrics column, bottom strip shows a grid of before/after road-to-turf placements.

idea.rationale: Re-defining the 'usability' of urban space from square metres to space-times by mining Google Maps' historic traffic patterns is a sharp data reframe — empty roads at 11pm are turf, even if they're cars at 9am. The modular set-up / dismantle mechanic makes the data insight a literal, repeatable product on the street.

strategy.rationale: Gatorade is PepsiCo's category-defining sports-hydration brand built on a 'fuel sport' mission, so a programme that unlocks where Indians can actually play is direct mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The context is data-backed and locally precise: Mumbai's 22M population on a constrained island geography is verifiable, and the 70% 'lack of space' barrier is the load-bearing insight (the board attributes it to Indian inactivity research). The data move depends on Google Maps' historic traffic dataset as the source of truth, which is a credible proprietary partner stack.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs through a Google Maps partnership for traffic data, the BMC (municipal government) for land allocation, and the Government of India for institutional weight — three layers of partner that move the work from media stunt to civic infrastructure. The modular turf system is the correct artefact for the time-bounded insight (roads need to revert to roads by morning) and locks the data-to-physical-reality loop. Press in Times of India, The Print and indiatimes carries the case into national conversation.

impact.rationale: The data-to-business chain is the right one: 150+ new turf locations identified by the Maps tool, 20+ allocated by the municipality, and '121% growth in volumes' as the business outcome for a hydration brand — the metric a beverage marketer cares about. Per RIGOUR pattern 1, 121% lacks an absolute base (121% of what baseline, over what comparison window, in what region?), which softens it from a hard sales proof to a directional uplift; the locational counts are absolute and credible, but the press coverage is entrant-reported and unaudited.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because historic Google Maps traffic data is converted into a literal city-scale play-finder, with municipal and national partners turning the dataset into 150+ new physical turfs — a Creative Data case where the data move becomes urban infrastructure, not just a campaign.
